translation: Those who disbelieve and avert [people] from the way of Allah - He will waste their deeds
commentary:
ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَصَدُّوا۟ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ أَضَلَّ أَعْمَـٰلَهُمْ

*Those who disbelieve and avert [people] from the way of Allah - He will waste their deeds.*

Surah Muhammad was revealed shortly before the Battle of Badr, making it one of two surahs that address this pivotal military engagement. The other is Surah Al-Baqarah, which granted the initial sanction for Muslims to fight in the path of Allah ﷻ. Surah Muhammad continues this theme but was designated as a separate revelation with its own profound name and structure.

The surah begins with a unique linguistic approach - it opens abruptly with a relative clause , الَّذِينَ (those who). This is unusual in the Quran, as most surahs begin with formalities: descriptions of Allah's ﷻ praise, declarations about the greatness of the Book, oaths to capture attention, or direct addresses to the audience. But Surah Muhammad launches immediately into its message, reflecting the military urgency of its content. Like a general entering to give direct orders rather than delivering an opening speech, the surah wastes no time on preliminaries. This structural choice reinforces why it's also known as Surah Al-Qital (the Surah of Fighting).

The historical backdrop is essential for understanding these opening ayahs. The Muslims of Makkah - the Muhajirun - had paid dearly for their faith. They endured insults, expulsion from families and homes, torture, beatings and even martyrdom. They understood viscerally what it meant to be Muslim and the sacrifices Islam demanded. The Muslims of Madinah - the Ansar - while overwhelmingly sincere, had not yet faced such trials. Their experience of Islam thus far had been beautiful and community-building, focused on spiritual growth and brotherhood.

When the Prophet ﷺ received revelation (not necessarily Quranic but through other forms of divine communication) instructing him to engage the Makkan army approaching for the Battle of Badr, it created tension. The Prophet ﷺ had already sent out eight small military campaigns, four involving him directly and four not. Even in those campaigns, he had initiated peace treaties with tribes along the way. The catalyzing moment came when one expedition was discovered, a skirmish broke out, and Abu Sufyan sent an urgent call for help. This prompted the Quraysh to dispatch an army of one thousand soldiers.

Despite having a choice, the Prophet ﷺ declared they should go to war and meet the approaching army. Some struggled with this decision. They understood the Muhajirun seeking to reclaim assets through raiding caravans - that seemed justified given the persecution they'd suffered. But all-out war? Was that necessary? After six months of relative peace, couldn't they negotiate? Some Muslims - not necessarily hypocrites but those new to the faith - questioned why military engagement was required. Wasn't Islam supposed to be about peace, good character toward neighbors, and excellence in conduct?

But the Prophet ﷺ had cultivated not merely a group but a nation. With a nation comes constitution, law and command structure. When the Messenger's ﷺ instruction came to advance to battle, it required obedience.

Allah ﷻ addresses this uncertainty immediately: الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَصَدُّوا۟ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ - "Those who have disbelieved and stopped themselves and others from the path of Allah." When you read "those who disbelieve," you think of the Makkans, of leaders like Abu Jahl and Abu Lahab. When you read "those who stop people from Allah's ﷻ path," you think of the Quraysh. But as the surah progresses, we discover Allah ﷻ is saying something more, encompassing a broader reality.

أَضَلَّ أَعْمَـٰلَهُمْ - "He has caused their deeds to be lost." Whatever advances the disbelievers were making toward the Muslims, whatever preparations they had undertaken, Allah ﷻ declares them already wasted. The battle hasn't even occurred, yet their efforts - both in this world and the next - are already nullified. This is a powerful assurance to believers facing overwhelming odds: the outcome is already decided in Allah's ﷻ favor.

translation: And those who believe and do righteous deeds and believe in what has been sent down upon Muhammad - and it is the truth from their Lord - He will remove from them their misdeeds and amend their condition
commentary:
وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ وَءَامَنُوا۟ بِمَا نُزِّلَ عَلَىٰ مُحَمَّدٍ وَهُوَ ٱلْحَقُّ مِن رَّبِّهِمْ ۙ كَفَّرَ عَنْهُمْ سَيِّـَٔاتِهِمْ وَأَصْلَحَ بَالَهُمْ

*And those who believe and do righteous deeds and believe in what has been sent down upon Muhammad - and it is the truth from their Lord - He will remove from them their misdeeds and amend their condition.*

In contrast to the disbelievers, Allah ﷻ describes those who believed and performed righteous deeds. When we read وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ (those who believed), this encompasses belief in everything: Allah ﷻ, the angels, all the prophets, and divine revelation. But then Allah ﷻ adds a specific clause: وَءَامَنُوا۟ بِمَا نُزِّلَ عَلَىٰ مُحَمَّدٍ - "and believed in what was sent down upon Muhammad."

The phrasing here is crucial. Allah ﷻ didn't say "believed in what was revealed" (al-Quran specifically), but rather مَا نُزِّلَ ( whatever was sent down). This encompasses not only the Quran but also the Sunnah, the hadith, and even the feelings and insights placed in the Prophet's ﷺ heart. Everything that came to him from Allah ﷻ is included in this belief.

This addresses a dangerous ideology that had emerged among the weaker Muslims and hypocrites. They argued that they should take the Quran seriously as the direct word of Allah ﷻ, but the Prophet ﷺ himself was merely the delivery mechanism. Therefore, if he had a personal opinion about something - like going to war - that was his individual initiative, not necessarily binding. They claimed to follow Allah ﷻ but not necessarily the Prophet ﷺ in his personal decisions.

The use of the name "Muhammad" here is remarkable. Of the four times this name appears in the Quran, three are typically accompanied by his title "Rasul" (Messenger). Allah ﷻ honors the Prophet ﷺ by mentioning his title alongside his name. Moreover, throughout the Quran, when Allah ﷻ addresses His Prophet ﷺ, He never calls him by his personal name as He did with other prophets - "O Adam," "O Ibrahim," "O Musa," "O Isa." Instead, He addresses him by his titles: "O Prophet," "O Messenger," "O one wrapped in garments." This is among the unique honors bestowed upon him.

In fact, when people came to the Prophet ﷺ and called out "Ya Muhammad!" a revelation came down warning them not to address him casually like that, lest their deeds be nullified. Yet ironically, today we make posters proclaiming "Ya Muhammad" - something Allah ﷻ never said in the Quran.

So why does this ayah use "Muhammad" without "Rasul"? Because "Rasul" means messenger - someone who delivers a message. The hypocrites said, "Yes, he's a messenger, and we appreciate him for delivering the message of the Quran. But him by himself, without that role, isn't worth following on his personal opinions."

Allah ﷻ responds by using the name Muhammad alone: "Whatever was sent down upon Muhammad" - him as a person, not just as a formal messenger. Whatever instructions he gives, whatever he speaks, especially the specific instruction being discussed here (let's go meet them in battle), that is وَهُوَ ٱلْحَقُّ مِن رَّبِّهِمْ - "the truth from their Lord."

For those who maintain this complete belief, كَفَّرَ عَنْهُمْ سَيِّـَٔاتِهِمْ - "He has buried away from them their sins." But the next phrase goes deeper: وَأَصْلَحَ بَالَهُمْ - "and He corrected their condition." The word بَال  refers to one's situation or state, but it also means development and molding. Through continued loyalty to Muhammad ﷺ and through the trials they face, Allah ﷻ molds their character. Over time, they become stronger people. He fixes not just their external circumstances but their internal condition, shaping them through each test they pass.

translation: That is because those who disbelieve follow falsehood, and those who believe follow the truth from their Lord. Thus does Allah present to the people their comparisons
commentary:
ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ ٱتَّبَعُوا۟ ٱلْبَـٰطِلَ وَأَنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱتَّبَعُوا۟ ٱلْحَقَّ مِن رَّبِّهِمْ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ يَضْرِبُ ٱللَّهُ لِلنَّاسِ أَمْثَـٰلَهُمْ

*That is because those who disbelieve follow falsehood, and those who believe follow the truth from their Lord. Thus does Allah present to the people their examples.*

Notice that Allah ﷻ uses the past tense: ٱتَّبَعُوا۟ ( they followed). He doesn't say "they follow" or "they will follow," but "they have followed." This is the final verdict. Those who disbelieved have proven through their actions that they followed الْبَـٰطِلَ ( falsehood). They are now a lost cause. The time for dialogue has ended; now is the time for confrontation.

Conversely, those who believed ٱتَّبَعُوا۟ ٱلْحَقَّ مِن رَّبِّهِمْ - "have proven themselves by following the truth from their Lord." They've demonstrated their commitment through their obedience to the Prophet's ﷺ instruction, even when it meant going to war against their own relatives.

كَذَٰلِكَ يَضْرِبُ ٱللَّهُ لِلنَّاسِ أَمْثَـٰلَهُمْ - "And that is how Allah strikes examples for people of their likes." The word أَمْثَـٰلَهُمْ ( their examples/likes) indicates that similar patterns have occurred in previous nations. Throughout history, Allah ﷻ has shown examples of believers who remained steadfast and disbelievers who persisted in falsehood. In all those historical accounts, Allah's ﷻ wrath eventually came upon the disbelievers. Those examples mean that the same will happen here - but this time, the companions of the Prophet ﷺ will themselves be the instrument of Allah's ﷻ divine retribution against the Quraysh.

translation: So when you meet those who disbelieve [in battle], strike [their] necks until, when you have inflicted slaughter upon them, then secure their bonds, and either [confer] favor afterwards or ransom [them] until the war lays down its burdens. That [is the command]. And if Allah had willed, He could have taken vengeance upon them [Himself], but [He ordered armed struggle] to test some of you by means of others. And those who are killed in the cause of Allah - never will He waste their deeds
commentary:
فَإِذَا لَقِيتُمُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ فَضَرْبَ ٱلرِّقَابِ حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَآ أَثْخَنتُمُوهُمْ فَشُدُّوا۟ ٱلْوَثَاقَ فَإِمَّا مَنًّۢا بَعْدُ وَإِمَّا فِدَآءً حَتَّىٰ تَضَعَ ٱلْحَرْبُ أَوْزَارَهَا ۚ ذَٰلِكَ وَلَوْ يَشَآءُ ٱللَّهُ لَٱنتَصَرَ مِنْهُمْ وَلَـٰكِن لِّيَبْلُوَا۟ بَعْضَكُم بِبَعْضٍ ۗ وَٱلَّذِينَ قُتِلُوا۟ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ فَلَن يُضِلَّ أَعْمَـٰلَهُمْ

*So when you meet those who disbelieve [in battle], strike [their] necks until, when you have inflicted slaughter upon them, then secure their bonds, and either [confer] favor afterwards or ransom [them] until the war lays down its burdens. That [is the command]. And if Allah had willed, He could have taken retribution from them [Himself], but [He ordered armed struggle] to test some of you by means of others. And those who are killed in the cause of Allah - never will He waste their deeds.*

The word لَقِيتُمُ  means to meet, but in Arabic it also carries the meaning of meeting in battle - making contact, engaging face to face in combat. فَضَرْبَ ٱلرِّقَابِ - "then strike the necks." There's an implied verb here that suggests decisive action: eliminate them, strike them down. This isn't a call for cruelty but for effectiveness in battle.

حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَآ أَثْخَنتُمُوهُمْ - "until you have thoroughly weakened them." The word أَثْخَنتُمُوهُمْ  literally refers to when blood has been shed abundantly and the warmth of blood is spilled everywhere. It means you've devastated the enemy forces in the battlefield, thoroughly defeating them.

فَشُدُّوا۟ ٱلْوَثَاقَ - "then tighten the bonds." Once captives are taken, don't treat them gently - secure their ropes and chains firmly. This instruction to take a tough stance applies even to prisoners of war. These aren't strangers - these are the uncles, cousins, fathers and brothers of the Muhajirun. This is a family reunion after six months of separation, but now on a battlefield.

The emotional weight of this cannot be overstated. Abdur Rahman ibn Awf (RA) later recounted to his father that during the battle, "You were in my crosshairs. I had pulled back my arrow and you were my target, but I thought, 'This is my father,' and I pulled back." Abu Bakr (RA) responded, "Good, because if you had come into my view, I would have shot you. I would have taken you out." Allah ﷻ is telling the Muslims that they cannot think of the disbelievers as family anymore. Just as Prophet Nuh (AS) was told about his son - "He is not of your family" - these blood ties must be severed when they conflict with faith. The most important ties are now the ties of belief and commitment to the Prophet ﷺ.

فَإِمَّا مَنًّۢا بَعْدُ وَإِمَّا فِدَآءً - "then either a favor afterwards or a ransom." After taking prisoners, you have two options: you can release them as a gesture of goodwill (mann), or you can demand ransom (fida). This phrase represents one of the more challenging interpretative moments in the Quran.

حَتَّىٰ تَضَعَ ٱلْحَرْبُ أَوْزَارَهَا ۚ - "until the war lays down its burdens." This phrase - أَوْزَارَهَا ( literally "its burdens" or "its arms") - creates two possible interpretations. 

The first interpretation: Don't take prisoners at all; simply eliminate the enemy until the entire war between Makkah and Madinah is finished. Until the conflict is completely resolved and the disbelievers are defeated, don't capture anyone - just fight.

The second interpretation: You do have the option to take prisoners, and you must engage in this war with a tough stance. But this war situation and your firm position against the disbelievers will continue until the final war ends, until war itself lays down its arms. Once the war is over, then you may exercise the options of releasing prisoners as a favor or taking ransom.

When the Battle of Badr actually occurred, the Prophet ﷺ took prisoners, indicating his understanding was the second interpretation - that taking prisoners was permissible. This is perfectly valid within the linguistic possibilities of the ayah. He also understood that once the war was won, they could implement the options for dealing with captives.

After the battle, the Prophet ﷺ consulted his companions about what to do with the prisoners. Abu Bakr (RA) suggested they should be freed - either by having them teach Muslims to read and write (those who were literate), or by taking ransom, or by releasing them as a gesture of goodwill. This would further demoralize the enemy: what kind of army has such high moral character that they release their captives?

Umar (RA) gave a different suggestion. He recommended executing all the prisoners because if released, they would just reinforce the enemy's army in future battles. Moreover, to ensure the loyalty of the Muslim ranks, he suggested that each Muslim execute his own relative among the captives - a true test of where their ultimate loyalty lay.

The Prophet ﷺ heard both options. Being رَحْمَةً لِّلْعَالَمِينَ (a mercy to all the worlds), which option did he choose? Abu Bakr's (RA) approach of mercy and strategic wisdom. He accepted ransom from some, freed others who would teach, and released some as goodwill gestures.

Later, a revelation came in Surah Al-Anfal (8:67) that addressed this decision, providing further divine guidance on the matter of prisoners of war. This demonstrates the dynamic nature of divine instruction - the ayah in Surah Muhammad came before the battle giving general parameters, and the ayah in Surah Al-Anfal came after, providing additional refinement based on what had occurred.

translation: He will guide them and amend their condition
commentary:
سَيَهْدِيهِمْ وَيُصْلِحُ بَالَهُمْ

سَيَهْدِيهِمْ—"Soon He will guide them." Even before the Day of Resurrection arrives, He will guide them straight to Jannah. These are the shuhada, the martyrs, who are taken early from this life but receive immediate divine guidance to their eternal reward.

وَيُصْلِحُ بَالَهُمْ—"And He will amend their condition." Allah ﷻ will fix all of their affairs. They won't be raised on the Day of Judgment injured, battered, or bleeding. Consider the example of Mus'ab ibn Umayr (RA), who was martyred at Uhud. He was once the fashion icon of Makkah and Madinah, always impeccably dressed in imported clothing, wearing the finest colognes. He was strikingly handsome and well-groomed. Yet he left all of that luxury behind for Islam. When he was martyred, there wasn't even enough cloth to properly shroud him—when they covered his face, his feet showed; when they covered his feet, his face was exposed. This is how he died for the sake of Allah ﷻ.

But Allah ﷻ promises that He will fix their state. The conditions of their death, no matter how difficult, will be transformed.

translation: And admit them to Paradise, which He has made known to them
commentary:
وَيُدْخِلُهُمُ ٱلْجَنَّةَ عَرَّفَهَا لَهُمْ

وَيُدْخِلُهُمُ ٱلْجَنَّةَ—"And admit them into Paradise"—but not just any admission. عَرَّفَهَا لَهُمْ—"which He has made known to them." He has thoroughly introduced it to them already.

Imagine this: As these blessed souls walk into Jannah, they look at a magnificent tree and say, "Oh, this is the Sidrat al-Muntaha that was mentioned!" They see the mansions and exclaim, "This is what the Quran described!" They observe the rivers and recognize, "These are the rivers that flowed in the verses!" Every promise they heard in the Quran, every description they memorized, suddenly becomes visible reality before their eyes. They match what they had been hearing with what they now see.

عَرَّفَهَا لَهُمْ captures this beautiful concept—Allah ﷻ already gave them the tour guide, the brochure, the preview through revelation. Now they're experiencing the fulfillment of every promise.

translation: O you who have believed, if you support Allah, He will support you and plant firmly your feet
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِن تَنصُرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ يَنصُرْكُمْ وَيُثَبِّتْ أَقْدَامَكُمْ

Now Allah ﷻ addresses the believers directly before the battle has even occurred. Keep in mind that at this point in the revelation, we don't yet know who will be martyred. But those who will be martyred are already being told about their introduction to Jannah.

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟—"O you who have believed"—those of you who claim to have iman. إِن تَنصُرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ—"If you support Allah"—if you help Allah's cause. يَنصُرْكُمْ—"He will support you." If you aid Allah, Allah will aid you.

This raises an important question: What does it mean to help Allah? Allah ﷻ is the One Who helped us; we saw this clearly at Badr when He says: وَلَقَدْ نَصَرَكُمُ ٱللَّهُ بِبَدْرٍ وَأَنتُمْ أَذِلَّةٌ—"And already had Allah given you victory at Badr while you were few" (3:123). So what does it mean for us to help Him?

Later, in Surah As-Saff, the Prophet Isa (AS) will ask: مَنْ أَنصَارِىٓ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ—"Who are my supporters for Allah?" (61:14). To help Allah means to help His Messenger in his mission, to make His religion a living reality.

This understanding is captured in a beautiful dua: اللَّهُمَّ انْصُرْ مَنْ نَصَرَ دِينَ مُحَمَّدٍ—"O Allah, help those who help the religion of Muhammad ﷺ."

We often ask Allah ﷻ for things—we ask for success, for protection, for the betterment of the Muslim Ummah. But when our businesses are contaminated with riba, when we're consuming haram, when there's lying, backbiting, and abuse in our families, when everything this deen stood for is being violated—what value does our supplication hold? How can we ask Allah ﷻ for help when we're not helping His deen? Allah ﷻ is teaching us a fundamental principle: if you want Allah's aid, you must first help this deen become a reality for people to see.

وَيُثَبِّتْ أَقْدَامَكُمْ—"And He will plant your feet firmly." He will make you steadfast; you will not run from the battle. This echoes the dua of the followers of Talut (Saul) when they faced the army of Jalut (Goliath): رَبَّنَآ أَفْرِغْ عَلَيْنَا صَبْرًا وَثَبِّتْ أَقْدَامَنَا—"Our Lord, pour upon us patience and plant firmly our feet" (2:250).

The imagery of صَبْرًا (patience) being poured is like a large container of fluid being emptied out onto someone—like the Gatorade tradition where they douse the championship winner. "Pour patience onto us, make our feet firm!" Because when they see those gigantic armies approaching, the natural human response might be to flee. They're asking Allah ﷻ: "Don't let us help ourselves to cowardice. Make our feet firm."

translation: But those who disbelieve - for them is misery, and He will waste their deeds
commentary:
وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ فَتَعْسًا لَّهُمْ وَأَضَلَّ أَعْمَـٰلَهُمْ

وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ فَتَعْسًا لَّهُمْ—"But those who disbelieve, for them is misfortune." The word تَعْس  carries multiple meanings: destruction, annihilation, destitution. It also describes someone who keeps stumbling with nowhere to go. 

There's profound irony here. The disbelievers made the Muslims destitute—they expelled them from their homes, took away their property, scattered their families. Yet Allah ﷻ declares that true destitution belongs to the disbelievers themselves.

وَأَضَلَّ أَعْمَـٰلَهُمْ—"And He will waste their deeds." This theme of wasted deeds appears repeatedly in this surah. Why? Because the Quraysh thought they were so righteous. They believed that taking care of the Sacred House, providing water to pilgrims, maintaining the Haram—that all of this justified everything else they did.

Allah ﷻ is crushing this mentality. You cannot think that because you do some good deeds, you're entitled to violate divine revelation, deny His Messenger ﷺ, and undermine His teachings. No. Your deeds will be rendered worthless.

What's striking is that up until this point, it seems Allah ﷻ is speaking only about the open disbelievers. But as we continue through the surah, we'll discover that this conversation has a double edge—Allah ﷻ is also addressing the hypocrites.

translation: That is because they disliked what Allah revealed, so He rendered worthless their deeds
commentary:
ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ كَرِهُوا۟ مَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ فَأَحْبَطَ أَعْمَـٰلَهُمْ

ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ كَرِهُوا۟ مَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ—"That is because they disliked what Allah revealed." Those are the people who detested what Allah ﷻ sent down. فَأَحْبَطَ أَعْمَـٰلَهُمْ—"So He rendered worthless their deeds." The word أَحْبَطَ means to cancel something away completely, to nullify it entirely.

Notice that the word أَعْمَال (deeds) in modern Arabic generally refers to good deeds unless qualified otherwise. Allah ﷻ is acknowledging that the disbelievers of Quraysh did perform some good actions. They took care of poor people. They provided hospitality to pilgrims. They maintained tribal bonds. They had admirable qualities—this is undeniable.

But here's the devastating reality: none of that will count if you hate revelation.

This answers a significant question that troubles many Muslim youth today. They observe their non-Muslim classmates and colleagues—Hindus, Buddhists, Jews, Christians, atheists—and notice that these are genuinely good people. They're charitable, kind, helpful, decent human beings. The question arises: "What's so bad about them?"

Allah's response is clear: There's one fundamental problem. It's not merely that they don't believe, but if they hate revelation—ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ كَرِهُوا۟ مَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ—then none of that good counts.

You don't get to decide what ultimate good is. Yes, we have some pre-programmed understanding of right and wrong inside us. We recognize certain moral truths intuitively. But the final verdict, the mature and complete understanding of what is truly good and truly bad, comes from Allah ﷻ through revelation.

Consider a child. A child thinks it knows what's good and bad. The parent tells the child to take medicine, and the child refuses, convinced it's bad. The parent insists it's good. The child has zero doubt in its own assessment, yet the parent knows better. What the parent is giving isn't an act of hatred—even though the child perceives it that way. "Why do you hate me so much? Why do you want me to drink that horrible stuff?"

The parent responds: "I know it's painful, but it's better for you. I hate seeing you in pain, but the pain of not giving you this medicine is far worse."

Imagine the anguish of parents who must put their children through chemotherapy. The pain they feel watching their child suffer is immense, but the pain of knowing what will happen without treatment is unbearable.

Similarly, Allah ﷻ asks us to do difficult things, even painful things. And if you think you feel the difficulty of it, understand that Allah ﷻ feels far more compassion for you than you can ever have for yourself. He loves you infinitely more than you can love yourself. But He asks you to do these things because they're better for you—whether you fully understand them or not.

People who refuse and hate what Allah ﷻ has revealed, who deny the love relationship they should have with their Creator—for them, the relationship is severed. فَأَحْبَطَ أَعْمَـٰلَهُمْ—their deeds are nullified.

translation: Have they not traveled through the land and seen how was the end of those before them? Allah destroyed [everything] over them, and for the disbelievers is something comparable
commentary:
أَفَلَمْ يَسِيرُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَيَنظُرُوا۟ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ ۚ دَمَّرَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهِمْ ۖ وَلِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ أَمْثَـٰلُهَا

أَفَلَمْ يَسِيرُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ—"Have they not traveled through the land?" This is a rhetorical question charged with incredulity. Haven't they gone around and observed? فَيَنظُرُوا۟ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ—"So they could see how was the end of those before them?"

The Quraysh were merchants. They traveled constantly for trade—to Syria, to Yemen, to distant lands. They passed by the ruins of destroyed civilizations: the remnants of 'Ad, Thamud, the people of Lut (AS). These weren't just stories; these were physical locations they witnessed with their own eyes on their trade routes.

دَمَّرَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهِمْ—"Allah destroyed them." The verb دَمَّرَ conveys complete devastation, utter ruin. Allah ﷻ brought crushing destruction upon them. وَلِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ أَمْثَـٰلُهَا—"And for the disbelievers are many similar examples."

It's not just those specific nations you've passed by. There are destroyed civilizations all over the planet, throughout history—nations that rose and fell, empires that crumbled. And they're all just like these people who reject divine guidance.

translation: That is because Allah is the protector of those who have believed and because the disbelievers have no protector
commentary:
ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ مَوْلَى ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَأَنَّ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ لَا مَوْلَىٰ لَهُمْ

ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ مَوْلَى ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟—"That is because Allah is the Protector of those who have believed." To understand the depth of this statement, we need to clarify the difference between two Arabic terms: وَلِيّ  and مَوْلَىٰ .

A وَلِيّ is someone who wants to protect you, someone looking out for you, someone who takes responsibility for you, someone who has protective feelings toward you. It's a sentiment, a relationship of care and guardianship.

A مَوْلَىٰ is someone who actively aids and protects you right now, someone presently defending you.

For example, your brother might be protective of you (wali) while living in another country—he feels protective, it's a sentiment he holds. But if he's in another country, he cannot be actively protecting you in the moment (mawla). Mawla is immediate, active protection and help.

In Surah Al-Baqarah, we read: ٱللَّهُ وَلِىُّ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟—"Allah is the Wali of those who believe" (2:257). And in the same ayah: وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ أَوْلِيَآؤُهُمُ ٱلطَّـٰغُوتُ—"But those who disbelieve, their allies are the false deities."

So in Surah Al-Baqarah, we have a clear parallel: we have an ally (wali) and they have allies (awliya). Allah ﷻ is our Wali, and their wali are the taghut—the rebellious authorities, the false gods, the leaders of kufr.

Now look at what happens in Surah Muhammad. Allah ﷻ says: ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ مَوْلَى ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟—"Allah is the Mawla of those who believed."

Based on the parallel with Surah Al-Baqarah, we would expect: "And the disbelievers, their mawla is so-and-so." But that's not what comes next.

وَأَنَّ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ لَا مَوْلَىٰ لَهُمْ—"And the disbelievers have no mawla whatsoever." None at all.

What does this mean? Allah ﷻ is teaching us that those who rebel—the leaders of Quraysh, the authorities of kufr, the powerful oppressors—they can be awliya (protective friends) for the disbelievers. They can have protective feelings toward them. But at the end of the day, they cannot be mawla for them. There is no one actually, actively protecting the enemies of Allah ﷻ when it truly matters.

This surah is about the Battle of Badr, but it also applies universally. Later at Uhud, when the Muslims suffered losses, Abu Sufyan (who had not yet accepted Islam) made a boastful proclamation praising Hubal, the false god: "Hubal be exalted and raised!" As the Muslims were climbing up the mountain, the Prophet ﷺ turned back and instructed them to respond: "Allahu Akbar!"—"Allah is Greater!" Greater than Hubal, greater than all false gods.

Then Abu Sufyan declared: "لَنَا الْعُزَّىٰ وَلَا عُزَّىٰ لَكُمْ—We have 'Uzza (another idol), and you have no 'Uzza!"

The Prophet ﷺ told the Muslims to respond: "اللَّهُ مَوْلَانَا وَلَا مَوْلَىٰ لَكُمْ—Allah is our Mawla, and you have no mawla!"

Notice the eloquence. The Prophet ﷺ didn't simply negate by saying "You have no Allah." Instead, he used their own statement structure against them with devastating precision: "You have no protector whatsoever."

This is the reality. وَأَنَّ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ لَا مَوْلَىٰ لَهُمْ—The disbelievers will have nobody actively protecting them when divine judgment comes.

translation: Indeed, Allah will admit those who have believed and done righteous deeds to gardens beneath which rivers flow, but those who disbelieve enjoy themselves and eat as grazing livestock eat, and the Fire will be a residence for them
commentary:
إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُدْخِلُ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ ۖ وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ يَتَمَتَّعُونَ وَيَأْكُلُونَ كَمَا تَأْكُلُ ٱلْأَنْعَـٰمُ وَٱلنَّارُ مَثْوًى لَّهُمْ

To understand the power of this ayah, we need to appreciate the economic context. There are two groups of Muslims in Madinah: the Muhajirun (migrants) and the Ansar (helpers). Both are in economic difficulty.

The Muhajirun left everything behind in Makkah—their businesses, their properties, their wealth. They arrived in Madinah with essentially nothing.

The Ansar weren't a wealthy community to begin with, and now they're being asked to share everything down the middle with an immigrant population. This is economically challenging for everyone involved.

Whenever an immigrant population arrives in large numbers—hundreds or thousands fleeing persecution—how does the host city typically respond? Throughout history, immigrants are placed in ghettos, marginalized areas. Politicians and the wealthy begin complaining: "Our neighborhoods are deteriorating because of these people. Why do we have these foreigners around? They're taking our jobs, consuming our resources. We barely have enough for ourselves!"

This mentality—that foreigners are polluting the city—is a recurring human response. Yet the Prophet ﷺ, within the first six months in Madinah, successfully created the system of brotherhood (mu'akhat) in which the Ansar and Muhajirun would share bread together, live together, sacrifice their businesses for one another. They truly helped each other despite the economic strain.

But the reality remains: both groups are suffering economically. The fact that they're going into battle with only eight swords and fewer than ten horses tells you how desperate their material situation is.

So what does Allah ﷻ offer these believers who have lost so much?

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُدْخِلُ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ—"Indeed, Allah will admit those who believe and do righteous deeds to gardens beneath which rivers flow."

"Look, you've lost a lot—I know. But where I'm taking you? You're going to be wealthy beyond imagination. You're going into gardens, and at the bottom of each garden, there will be rivers flowing."

Picture this: gardens laid out on top of hills, and when you look down the hillside, there's a beautiful river flowing beneath. Or imagine a waterfall cascading from the gardens. These are images of the most stunning vacation destinations on earth—but these gardens are eternal, perfect, beyond any worldly comparison.

Then comes the devastating contrast:

وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ يَتَمَتَّعُونَ—"But those who disbelieve enjoy themselves." Right now, they're having a great time. They're living it up, indulging in worldly pleasures without restraint.

وَيَأْكُلُونَ كَمَا تَأْكُلُ ٱلْأَنْعَـٰمُ—"And they eat as grazing livestock eat." How does cattle eat? Cattle chews and chews endlessly, with no manners whatsoever. It eats directly off the ground, consuming whatever it can find without discrimination or restriction.

Allah ﷻ compares the disbelievers to cattle when they eat. These people eat like animals—they have no reservations about what they consume. No boundaries, no limits, no higher purpose.

They live a hedonistic lifestyle, a purely animalistic existence. Animals have urges and immediately act on them. A bird sees a female bird and chases. A monkey sees a female monkey and pursues. That's what animals do—pure instinct, no restraint.

The disbelievers are hungry, so they eat whatever—no restrictions. They have desires, so they fulfill them—no boundaries. This is their animal life, and they consider it the height of pleasure. But the believers? They'll have gardens, rivers, sophisticated eternal rewards beyond imagination.

وَٱلنَّارُ مَثْوًى لَّهُمْ—"And the Fire is a residence for them." Mathwa indicates a place where they will remain for a very long time, a place they won't be able to escape from.

translation: And how many a city was stronger than your city [Makkah] which drove you out? We destroyed them; and there was no helper for them
commentary:
وَكَأَيِّن مِّن قَرْيَةٍ هِىَ أَشَدُّ قُوَّةً مِّن قَرْيَتِكَ ٱلَّتِىٓ أَخْرَجَتْكَ أَهْلَكْنَـٰهُمْ فَلَا نَاصِرَ لَهُمْ

As the coalition prepares to march against the believers, Allah ﷻ offers profound reassurance to the Prophet ﷺ and the Muslims. The message is clear: you are not alone, and this threat you face is not unprecedented.

The ayah addresses the Prophet ﷺ directly, referring to "your city that expelled you"—Makkah. This personal reference emphasizes the deep connection between the Messenger ﷺ and the city that rejected him. Yet Allah ﷻ reminds him that many nations before, far stronger than Makkah, met their destruction when they opposed their messengers.

The phrase "stronger in force" (أَشَدُّ قُوَّةً) indicates that these previous civilizations possessed greater military might, more resources and superior numbers compared to Makkah. Despite all their worldly power, Allah ﷻ destroyed them completely. The final phrase "they have no helper" (فَلَا نَاصِرَ لَهُمْ) emphasizes their total abandonment. No backup arrived, no reinforcements came and no alliance could save them from the consequences of their disbelief.

This serves as both comfort for the believers and a warning to their enemies: no amount of worldly strength can protect those who oppose the truth.

translation: So is he who is on clear evidence from his Lord like him to whom the evil of his work has been made attractive and they follow their [own] desires
commentary:
أَفَمَن كَانَ عَلَىٰ بَيِّنَةٍ مِّن رَّبِّهِۦ كَمَن زُيِّنَ لَهُۥ سُوٓءُ عَمَلِهِۦ وَٱتَّبَعُوٓا۟ أَهْوَآءَهُم

Allah ﷻ now presents a powerful contrast between two fundamentally different types of people. On one side stands the person who is على بينة (upon clear proof) from his Master—someone whose understanding of good and evil is rooted in divine revelation, not personal whim or cultural conditioning.

On the other side are those "for whom their evil deeds have been beautified" (زُيِّنَ لَهُۥ سُوٓءُ عَمَلِهِۦ). This beautification is crucial to understand. These individuals are not simply doing evil while knowing it is evil. Rather, their minds have been so twisted that they genuinely believe their wickedness is righteous.

Consider the Quraysh leaders who opposed the Prophet ﷺ. In their minds and in their rhetoric to the people of Makkah, they presented themselves as protectors of civilization. They claimed the Messenger ﷺ threatened their righteous religion and would corrupt families. They convinced themselves that by opposing Muhammad ﷺ, they were defending their nation and preserving their values. This delusion—that hatred of Islam was actually love of country—had been beautified in their minds.

The ayah concludes by noting that "they follow their empty whims" (وَٱتَّبَعُوٓا۟ أَهْوَآءَهُم). This is the foundation of their error. They have no real proof, no divine guidance and no objective standard. Everything flows from their desires, prejudices and what feels right to them in the moment.

The comparison is stark: clear proof from the Master versus decorated delusion. Divine guidance versus human whims. Truth versus self-deception.

translation: Is the description of Paradise, which the righteous are promised, wherein are rivers of water unaltered, rivers of milk the taste of which never changes, rivers of wine delicious to those who drink, and rivers of purified honey, in which they will have from all [kinds of] fruits and forgiveness from their Lord, like [that of] those who abide eternally in the Fire and are given to drink scalding water that will sever their intestines
commentary:
مَّثَلُ ٱلْجَنَّةِ ٱلَّتِى وُعِدَ ٱلْمُتَّقُونَ ۖ فِيهَآ أَنْهَـٰرٌ مِّن مَّآءٍ غَيْرِ ءَاسِنٍ وَأَنْهَـٰرٌ مِّن لَّبَنٍ لَّمْ يَتَغَيَّرْ طَعْمُهُۥ وَأَنْهَـٰرٌ مِّنْ خَمْرٍ لَّذَّةٍ لِّلشَّـٰرِبِينَ وَأَنْهَـٰرٌ مِّنْ عَسَلٍ مُّصَفًّى ۖ وَلَهُمْ فِيهَا مِن كُلِّ ٱلثَّمَرَٰتِ وَمَغْفِرَةٌ مِّن رَّبِّهِمْ ۖ كَمَنْ هُوَ خَـٰلِدٌ فِى ٱلنَّارِ وَسُقُوا۟ مَآءً حَمِيمًا فَقَطَّعَ أَمْعَآءَهُمْ

After contrasting two types of people, Allah ﷻ now contrasts their ultimate destinations. The ayah begins with "the example of Jannah"—Allah ﷻ is offering us a glimpse, just a taste of what awaits those who protect themselves through taqwa.

The description begins with rivers—not just any rivers, but rivers of different varieties, each perfect in its own way. First, rivers of water "that does not go stale" (غَيْرِ ءَاسِنٍ). This might seem simple until we reflect on our experience with water in this world. Water left standing becomes undrinkable. Even refrigerated water can develop an unpleasant taste over time. But in Jannah, the water remains perpetually fresh, pure and delightful.

Next come rivers of milk "whose taste has not changed" (لَّمْ يَتَغَيَّرْ طَعْمُهُۥ). Anyone who has left milk out knows what happens—it spoils rapidly, becoming sour and inedible. In Jannah, the milk never undergoes this degradation. It remains in its perfect state, always fresh, always delicious.

Then Allah ﷻ mentions rivers of wine, but notice the unique description: خَمْرٍ لَّذَّةٍ لِّلشَّـٰرِبِينَ. The word لَّذَّةٍ means "pleasure" or "delight," but the grammatical structure here is profound. Instead of the expected feminine form لَّذَّةٍ to match خَمْرٍ, Allah ﷻ uses a form that suggests this wine IS pleasure itself. It's as if you've never truly experienced taste until you've had this. Unlike worldly wine, which is an acquired taste that people often find bitter or harsh until they become accustomed to it, the wine of Jannah is pure delight for those who drink it.

Finally, rivers of honey "perfectly purified" (مُّصَفًّى)—clear, crystal pure, without the slightest imperfection.

But why these specific items? Why do rivers of honey and milk and pure water appeal to us? The answer reveals something profound about human nature. Allah ﷻ has imprinted the longing for Jannah into our very psyche. Even those who never read the Quran find themselves naturally drawn to these elements.

Consider how humanity universally responds to certain things. We love gardens. Green cities attract more tourists and are considered more beautiful. As people age, they often develop a passion for gardening—approaching death, they instinctively want to be near gardens. We seek out beaches and rivers when we want peace. The sight of sunlight sparkling on water soothes us. We associate fruits and vegetables with health and longevity.

Even in our artificial world, we try to recreate these elements. The very wealthy build fountains of drinks at their parties. Expensive restaurants display glasses upside down. Elite gatherings feature young servers in formal attire bringing trays of food and drink. All of this echoes the descriptions of Jannah, not because people read the Quran and copied it, but because Allah ﷻ placed this yearning within us.

We are descendants of Adam (AS), who tasted Jannah firsthand. Something of that experience has been passed down through generations, embedded in our collective memory. Everything Allah ﷻ describes, we naturally desire, down to the smallest details.

The ayah continues: "and for them therein are all kinds of fruits and forgiveness from their Master." After describing all these physical delights, Allah ﷻ mentions forgiveness. This placement is significant—the greatest blessing is not the rivers or fruits, but the forgiveness of Allah ﷻ, His pleasure and His mercy.

Then comes the devastating comparison: "Can he be compared to one who will abide eternally in the Fire?" The implied answer is obvious—no comparison is possible. While one group enjoys multiple varieties of perfect drinks, the other group "is given to drink scalding water that tears their intestines."

The word حَمِيمًا refers to water that has reached the highest degree of heat possible. When they drink this boiling water, it doesn't just burn—it tears open their veins from the inside. Imagine pouring acid on tissue and watching it disintegrate. That's what happens internally to those who drink this punishment. If drinking coffee that's too hot makes you feel the burn without actually damaging you, imagine liquid so hot it literally rips you apart from within.

The contrast is complete: wonderful drinks of multiple flavors versus torture through drink. Perfect pleasure versus perfect torment.

translation: And among them, [O Muhammad], are those who listen to you, until when they depart from you, they say to those who were given knowledge, "What has he said just now?" Those are the ones of whom Allah has sealed over their hearts and who have followed their [own] desires
commentary:
وَمِنْهُم مَّن يَسْتَمِعُ إِلَيْكَ حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا خَرَجُوا۟ مِنْ عِندِكَ قَالُوا۟ لِلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْعِلْمَ مَاذَا قَالَ ءَانِفًا ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ طَبَعَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِهِمْ وَٱتَّبَعُوٓا۟ أَهْوَآءَهُمْ

The narrative now shifts to expose a particular type of hypocrite. Allah ﷻ describes people who sit in the gatherings of the Prophet ﷺ, listening attentively to every word. They appear engaged, they seem focused and they give every indication of being serious students of knowledge.

But their true nature reveals itself when they leave. The phrase حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا خَرَجُوا۟ مِنْ عِندِكَ ("until when they leave your company") uses a word for closeness—عِندِكَ—that appears throughout the Quran to describe intimate proximity. When Allah ﷻ speaks of being close to disbelievers to witness their actions, He uses this same word. When the Quran describes the Sahaba's closeness to the Prophet ﷺ, this language appears. These hypocrites were sitting right there, in the closest positions.

Think about the practical reality: there were no microphone systems in those days. When the Prophet ﷺ spoke, whoever sat closest could hear best. These hypocrites positioned themselves in the front row, in the best spots for hearing every word clearly.

Then they leave and seek out "those who were given knowledge"—the recognized scholars among the Companions (RA). And they ask: "What did he just say?" (مَاذَا قَالَ ءَانِفًا). The word ءَانِفًا means "just now" or "moments ago."

But here's the question: if they were sitting right next to the Prophet ﷺ, hearing him clearly, why would they need to ask what he said? They could have asked him directly if they didn't understand. They waited until they left his presence, and then they went fishing for validation of their doubts.

This is the psychology of someone looking for an ally in their dissent. It's like saying to someone, "Can you believe what he just said?" hoping they'll respond, "I know, that was crazy, right?" They want someone to jump on the bandwagon with them, to confirm their concerns and validate their resistance. But they frame it cleverly: "What did he say? I don't think I heard correctly, because if I heard what I think I heard, that's a big problem."

Notice the context—this is happening as they're heading toward battle. They've already left Madinah and are on the way to engage the enemy. This is the worst possible time for a soldier to express doubt or uncertainty. In battle, hesitation means death. These hypocrites are deliberately creating dissent among the ranks at the most critical moment.

Allah ﷻ explains their condition: "Those are the ones upon whose hearts Allah has placed a seal, and they follow their empty desires." The phrase وَٱتَّبَعُوٓا۟ أَهْوَآءَهُمْ (they follow their desires) is identical to what was said earlier about the disbelievers. This is no accident. Allah ﷻ is making clear that the hypocrites within the ranks are fundamentally the same as the enemies on the battlefield. Both groups follow their whims rather than divine guidance.

This is why later in the Quran, Allah ﷻ commands: "Strive against the disbelievers and the hypocrites" (جَاهِدِ الْكُفَّارَ وَالْمُنَافِقِينَ)—putting them together as equal threats to the Muslim community.

translation: And those who are guided - He increases them in guidance and gives them their righteousness
commentary:
وَٱلَّذِينَ ٱهْتَدَوْا۟ زَادَهُمْ هُدًى وَءَاتَىٰهُمْ تَقْوَىٰهُمْ

In stark contrast to the hypocrites, Allah ﷻ now describes those who have truly committed to guidance. These are the believers who don't fall for the propaganda, who don't entertain the doubts planted by the hypocrites and who remain firm despite the whispers of dissent around them.

For these people, Allah ﷻ increases them even more in guidance (زَادَهُمْ هُدًى). This is one of the fundamental principles of spiritual growth: guidance begets more guidance. When you follow the truth you have, Allah ﷻ opens up more truth to you. When you act on the knowledge you possess, Allah ﷻ grants you deeper understanding.

Then comes a remarkable phrase: وَءَاتَىٰهُمْ تَقْوَىٰهُمْ—"and He gave them their taqwa." Notice it says "their taqwa"—the taqwa that already belonged to them. This teaches us something profound about human potential. As Allah ﷻ says elsewhere: "And He inspired the soul with its wickedness and its taqwa" (وَنَفْسٍ وَمَا سَوَّاهَا فَأَلْهَمَهَا فُجُورَهَا وَتَقْوَاهَا). Within every human being exists both potential for evil and potential for good. We have both capacities embedded within us.

The taqwa was always there, latent, waiting. But Allah ﷻ activated it. He pressed the "on" button, so to speak. He granted them access to the protective awareness that was already part of their spiritual makeup. And what triggered this activation? Simply their refusal to listen to the propaganda of the hypocrites. Simply their commitment to stay firm.

This is the mercy and justice of Allah ﷻ: when you take one step toward Him, He takes multiple steps toward you. When you resist the whispers of doubt, He strengthens your certainty.

translation: Then do they await except that the Hour should come upon them unexpectedly? But already there have come [some of] its indications. Then what good to them, when it has come, will be their remembrance
commentary:
فَهَلْ يَنظُرُونَ إِلَّا ٱلسَّاعَةَ أَن تَأْتِيَهُم بَغْتَةً ۖ فَقَدْ جَآءَ أَشْرَاطُهَا ۚ فَأَنَّىٰ لَهُمْ إِذَا جَآءَتْهُمْ ذِكْرَىٰهُمْ

Allah ﷻ now addresses the hypocrites and disbelievers with a piercing question: Are they waiting around for the Hour itself to arrive before they believe? Do they need to see the Day of Judgment with their own eyes before they take this message seriously?

The Hour will come suddenly (بَغْتَةً)—without warning, without preparation time. But here's the critical point: "Its signs have already come" (فَقَدْ جَآءَ أَشْرَاطُهَا). The word أَشْرَاطُهَا refers to the indicators and signs of the Day of Judgment. And what is the greatest sign? The Prophet Muhammad ﷺ himself.

The arrival of the final Messenger ﷺ, the revelation of the final Book and the establishment of the final message—these are among the clearest indications that the Hour is approaching. If these signs don't wake a person up, what will?

Allah ﷻ asks: فَأَنَّىٰ لَهُمْ إِذَا جَآءَتْهُمْ ذِكْرَىٰهُمْ—"How will they benefit from their reminder when it comes to them?" If the Quran doesn't remind them now, if the Prophet ﷺ doesn't wake them now, then what good will remembrance do when the Hour actually arrives? When the Day of Judgment comes, remembrance becomes useless. Belief becomes meaningless. The time for action will have passed.

This ayah serves as both warning and motivation. The signs are here. The reminder has come. The greatest proof stands before you. Don't wait until it's too late to benefit from it.

translation: So know, [O Muhammad], that there is no deity except Allah and ask forgiveness for your sin and for the believing men and believing women. And Allah knows of your movement and your resting place
commentary:
فَٱعْلَمْ أَنَّهُۥ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا ٱللَّهُ وَٱسْتَغْفِرْ لِذَنۢبِكَ وَلِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَٱلْمُؤْمِنَـٰتِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ مُتَقَلَّبَكُمْ وَمَثْوَىٰكُمْ

The surah now returns to address the Prophet ﷺ directly, but through him, addresses the entire community. The command فَٱعْلَمْ (so know) means to know completely, to refresh your understanding, to return to the fundamentals. It's as if Allah ﷻ is saying: in light of everything discussed—the threats from enemies, the hypocrites within your ranks, the coming Hour—go back to the foundation. Know with absolute certainty that لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا ٱللَّهُ—there is no deity worthy of worship except Allah ﷻ.

This message reaches the hypocrites through the Prophet ﷺ. It's telling them: you need to renew your commitment. You need to take your shahadah again. Return to the basics of faith.

Then comes the command: وَٱسْتَغْفِرْ لِذَنۢبِكَ—"and seek forgiveness for your dhanb." This word dhanb requires careful understanding. It comes from dhanaab, which means the tail of an animal. In Arabic usage, dhanb refers to something that may not be evil in itself, but whose consequences are undesirable—just as a tail isn't necessarily bad, but what comes from it is unpleasant.

This is crucially different from sayyi'ah, which refers to something evil and ugly in its very nature—lying, stealing, murder. A dhanb is something that might be neutral or even good in intention, but its results turn out to be problematic.

In the context of battle and strategy, this becomes clear. Sometimes the Prophet ﷺ would make decisions based on his own judgment—not from direct revelation, but from his human intellect and wisdom. As the most intelligent and wise human being ever to live, we must believe that any decision he made at the human level was the best possible decision any person could make in that situation.

But who has the authority to say a better decision was possible? Only Allah ﷻ. Only divine knowledge can correct even the best human judgment. This happened after the Battle of Badr when the Prophet ﷺ, consulting with his Companions (RA), decided to accept ransom and release the prisoners. At the human level, this was an excellent decision based on sound reasoning. But Allah ﷻ revealed that a different course of action would have been better. Only Allah ﷻ could make that assessment.

Notice that dhanb is always mentioned in the singular for the Prophet ﷺ. Allah ﷻ never says "your sins" plural. It's always "your dhanb"—as if to say there might be one thing, perhaps something you're uncertain about, some decision that concerns you. Make istighfar for that.

Another dimension of dhanb is that it refers to something potentially embarrassing. The Prophet ﷺ, even when he has done nothing wrong, even when he has made the best possible decision without violating any revelation, still feels embarrassed before Allah ﷻ. Why? Because he expects so much more from himself. He holds himself to such a high standard that even the slightest imperfection troubles him.

This teaches us about true leadership. When a general loses a battle, who is blamed—the soldiers or the general? Even if the soldiers made mistakes, even if they didn't follow instructions properly, the general takes responsibility. When a store's sales decline, the CEO doesn't question each employee—he questions the manager.

This is what Allah ﷻ is teaching through this command to the Prophet ﷺ: وَٱسْتَغْفِرْ لِذَنۢبِكَ وَلِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَٱلْمُؤْمِنَـٰتِ—"Seek forgiveness for your dhanb and for the believing men and believing women." As a leader, own the mistakes of those you lead. Consider their shortcomings as your responsibility. When your followers make errors, don't point fingers at them—seek forgiveness on their behalf. Take ownership.

This is the leadership model of prophethood, and through the Prophet ﷺ, Allah ﷻ teaches us what true leadership looks like. When your employees make mistakes, when your volunteers mess up, when those under your authority fall short—it's not their mistake, it's yours. You weren't enough of a leader. Allah ﷻ tells the man who never made a mistake in leadership to take ownership of any mistakes his followers might make.

The ayah specifically mentions both believing men and believing women (وَلِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَٱلْمُؤْمِنَـٰتِ). This is profound in the context of battle. Usually, discussions of warfare don't prominently feature women. In classical times, women didn't typically engage in combat. Yet Allah ﷻ makes a point in this surah—and in the next surah about the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah, where virtually no women were present except the Prophet's wife—to specifically mention believing women.

Why? Because Allah ﷻ is teaching the Muslim Ummah a critical lesson: when you go into battle, your wives are in battle too. Your mothers are in battle too. It's not only a physical battle for the men—it's a psychological and emotional battle for the women. They must battle their own hearts to let their loved ones go.

Consider the immense sacrifice: mothers letting their sons go, wives letting their husbands go, daughters letting their fathers go, sisters letting their brothers go. These men were their protectors, their providers, the heads of their households. In Madinah's context, women were deeply dependent on these men for their security and provision. Yet they let them go for the sake of Allah ﷻ.

If the believing men think they're exposed to danger on the open battlefield, the believing women are equally exposed in Madinah. They face different dangers—the danger of loss, of abandonment, of having to survive without their protectors. They're in difficulty like they've never experienced before.

By mentioning both men and women together in this context, Allah ﷻ honors the complete sacrifice of the believing community. Both genders are in this struggle together, each facing their own form of jihad, each deserving of recognition and forgiveness.

The ayah concludes: وَٱللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ مُتَقَلَّبَكُمْ وَمَثْوَىٰكُمْ—"And Allah knows your movements and your place of rest." The word مُتَقَلَّبَكُمْ  comes from the root for turning over or changing. It refers to your constantly changing situation, your movements from place to place, the evolving challenges you face.

This dunya is characterized by constant change—mutaqallib. Hearts turn over, situations shift, circumstances evolve. Allah ﷻ knows every twist and turn you go through. And He knows your mathwa—your final resting place, where you will eventually settle.

This comprehensive knowledge should bring us both comfort and consciousness. Comfort, because Allah ﷻ knows exactly what we're going through at every moment. And consciousness, because nothing we do—no movement, no decision, no struggle—escapes His knowledge. He sees it all, He knows it all and He will account for it all with perfect justice.

translation: Those who believe say, "Why has a surah not been sent down? But when a precise surah is revealed and fighting is mentioned therein, you see those in whose hearts is hypocrisy looking at you with a look of one overcome by death. And more appropriate for them [would have been]
commentary:
وَيَقُولُ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَوْلَا نُزِّلَتْ سُورَةٌ ۖ فَإِذَآ أُنزِلَتْ سُورَةٌ مُّحْكَمَةٌ وَذُكِرَ فِيهَا ٱلْقِتَالُ ۙ رَأَيْتَ ٱلَّذِينَ فِى قُلُوبِهِم مَّرَضٌ يَنظُرُونَ إِلَيْكَ نَظَرَ ٱلْمَغْشِىِّ عَلَيْهِ مِنَ ٱلْمَوْتِ ۖ فَأَوْلَىٰ لَهُمْ

The believers would say, "How come no surah has come down?" This statement carries multiple layers of meaning. On one level, it could reflect genuine believers expressing their anticipation for divine guidance. On another level, it reveals the pressure the believers faced from constant criticism by the disbelievers who mocked the Prophet ﷺ, saying he wanted to go to battle on his own authority without any revelation supporting him.

The believers might have thought that if only a clear surah would be revealed addressing this matter, it would end the controversy and silence the critics. The word نُزِّلَتْ  implies the complete revelation of something, as opposed to gradual revelation. Even if just a portion of a verdict came down, it would resolve this problem decisively.

Then, when a  سورة محكمة  - meaning firm and complete in its commandments - was finally revealed, and combat was mentioned within it, you would see those who have a disease in their hearts staring at you with a particular look. This wasn't a look of relief or conviction. Rather, it was the look of someone overcome by death - the expression of someone passing out, facing their end. After campaigning so hard that the Prophet ﷺ was making things up on his own, now that an actual surah had been revealed, they couldn't maintain their facade. The revelation exposed their hypocrisy.

The ayah ends with فَأَوْلَىٰ لَهُمْ  - a curse in Arabic, essentially meaning "then they should be cursed" or "woe to them."

translation: Obedience and good words. And when the matter [of fighting] was determined, if they had been true to Allah, it would have been better for them
commentary:
طَاعَةٌ وَقَوْلٌ مَّعْرُوفٌ ۚ فَإِذَا عَزَمَ ٱلْأَمْرُ فَلَوْ صَدَقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ لَكَانَ خَيْرًا لَّهُمْ

Allah ﷻ states what is truly required: طَاعَةٌ (obedience) and قَوْلٌ مَّعْرُوفٌ (decent speech). Notice this isn't even a complete sentence - just the essential elements. Obedience is necessary. If you're going to speak, speak with proper protocol and decency. If you have strategic suggestions about the battle - perhaps a tactical advantage or a better position - present them respectfully within appropriate bounds.

Then, when the decision has been affirmed and finalized, when the matter has been resolved, the ayah says: فَلَوْ صَدَقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ لَكَانَ خَيْرًا لَّهُمْ (had they been truthful to Allah, it would have been better for them). What's remarkable here is that we might have expected "had they been truthful to the Messenger," since the Messenger ﷺ was the one calling them to fight. But Allah ﷻ says "truthful to Allah," teaching us that being true to the Messenger ﷺ is being true to Allah ﷻ. Had they confirmed their faith through truthfulness to Allah ﷻ, it would have been infinitely better for them.

translation: So would you perhaps, if you turned away, cause corruption on earth and sever your [ties of] relationship
commentary:
فَهَلْ عَسَيْتُمْ إِن تَوَلَّيْتُمْ أَن تُفْسِدُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَتُقَطِّعُوٓا۟ أَرْحَامَكُمْ

This is a tremendously heavy ayah with profound implications. The word تَوَلَّيْتُمْ  has two distinct meanings in Arabic. The first meaning is "to turn away" or "to turn your backs." The second meaning is "to take control" or "to take command" - as in taking the reins of power.

According to the first interpretation, Allah ﷻ is warning the Muslims about to enter battle: Is it possible that if you turn away from the battlefield, you will cause corruption in the earth? If you don't engage these enemy forces who are spreading their ideology like a cancer through society, there will be consequences. The corruption will spread until it even enters households and cuts family relations. The evil that goes unchallenged doesn't remain contained - it seeps into the most intimate bonds, destroying families from within.

However, there's another interpretation that carries an even more striking message. If we take تَوَلَّيْتُمْ to mean "taking control," then Allah ﷻ is addressing something far more universal: You Muslims right now exist as a mixture - true believers, weak believers, and hypocrites are among you. In this state, if you take power and control over the region, it's dangerous. Muslims must be a purified and cleansed nation before they assume positions of power. If you gain political control while you haven't been cleansed as a people, while the ranks of hypocrites lie hidden among you - hypocrites who are often more power-hungry than anyone else - then what will become of the land? Corruption will spread in the name of religion, and the true religion at that.

This interpretation offers one of the Quran's most significant statements about the relationship between religious purity and political power. Sociologists and critics often point to organized religion as a source of historical oppression and corruption, and they have a point. It's so valid that Allah ﷻ Himself warned us about it. Muslims, if you're not cleansed as a people and you come to power prematurely, you too will cause corruption in the land. And this corruption won't remain at the governmental level - وَتُقَطِّعُوٓا۟ أَرْحَامَكُمْ (you will cut family relations). When corruption exists at the political level with those in power, it trickles down into the depths of society, penetrating even the private walls of homes.

Why does Allah ﷻ place this warning at this particular juncture? Because going into battle serves as a purification process for the Muslim community. It clearly distinguishes who is a hypocrite and who is not. People won't risk their lives unless they're truly convinced of this message and convinced of the paradise Allah ﷻ has promised with its rivers and eternal bliss. Those who refuse to participate expose themselves. Battle, in this context, becomes the filter that separates genuine Muslims from hypocrites who otherwise remain inseparable within the community.

This has immediate relevance for us today. Look at our institutions - even at the level of a single mosque in America, many feel like microcosms of corrupt Muslim governments. People assume power and refuse to relinquish it, ignoring the community's voice, engaging in backroom deals and even lawsuits. If we can't establish peace, harmony, justice, fairness, transparency and accountability at the level of a small organization - just dozens of people - how can we ever speak seriously about ruling lands with Islamic law?

The problem exists at every level. At the household level, there's unfairness from heads of households toward children and wives, violations of mahr agreements, daughters denied their rightful inheritance. This individual corruption scales up to organizational corruption, then institutional corruption. When Muslim nations are corrupt, who gets blamed? Islam does. When Muslim organizations behave corruptly, Islam's reputation suffers. The religion that came to remove evil from the world becomes accused of evil because its ambassadors are evil.

This ayah doesn't absolve us from the responsibility to establish justice on earth. But it does instill in us the absolute necessity of cleansing ourselves first. We must get our act together. We cannot have those high-level conversations about Islamic governance until the very foundational elements are in order.

translation: Those [who do so] are the ones that Allah has cursed, so He deafened them and blinded their vision
commentary:
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ لَعَنَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ فَأَصَمَّهُمْ وَأَعْمَىٰٓ أَبْصَـٰرَهُمْ

Those are the people Allah ﷻ cursed - those who take the reins of power and then create corruption in His name. فَأَصَمَّهُمْ (He deafened them) - He made them unable to hear. وَأَعْمَىٰٓ أَبْصَـٰرَهُمْ (and He blinded their eyes).

Corrupt Muslim leaders become completely deaf and blind. They no longer hear the cries of their people or their pleas for justice. They don't see the blood they're shedding - it means nothing to them, as insignificant as killing insects. Look at what happens in parts of the Muslim world: rulers cannot see the blood they're spilling. People are being hauled into prison while reciting ayat from the Quran about how the angels will question them and how Allah ﷻ will hold them accountable, yet the soldiers don't care. They've been deafened and blinded. Nothing penetrates anymore.

translation: Then do they not reflect upon the Qur'an, or are there locks upon [their] hearts
commentary:
أَفَلَا يَتَدَبَّرُونَ ٱلْقُرْءَانَ أَمْ عَلَىٰ قُلُوبٍ أَقْفَالُهَآ

Don't they deeply reflect on the Quran? The word يَتَدَبَّرُونَ comes from دُبُر , meaning "the back." تَدَبُّر means looking at what's behind something, examining its depth. When you look at the ocean, you wonder what life exists beneath the surface. When Allah ﷻ says something, تَدَبُّر means thinking about the implications behind His words, the consequences of what He's saying.

"Reflection" and "pondering" only capture one dimension of this word. التَدَبُّر means examining something from every angle - checking it backwards and forwards, investigating its reality thoroughly. It's like when someone tries to sell you a counterfeit phone that looks real from the front, but when you check the back, you discover it's just paper. التَدَبُّر is checking the reality of something, and that takes time. It requires dedicated study of the Quran, reflecting on it, thinking deeply about how it applies to your life.

But some people don't have time for that because their hearts are busy elsewhere. أَمْ عَلَىٰ قُلُوبٍ أَقْفَالُهَآ (Or are there locks upon their hearts?) Notice the shift from the previous discussion about deafening and blinding to now discussing locks on hearts. Earlier we saw طَبَعَ ٱللَّهُ (Allah sealed), but now we see أَقْفَالُهَآ (their own locks). These hearts came with built-in locks that are currently open. But if you don't take advantage of the opening, those locks will activate and close. There's no external lock being applied - it's already there, waiting. This is the فِطْرَة (natural disposition) inside the human being: the lock is already present.

What this ayah reveals from another perspective is remarkable. Reflection and careful thinking are exercises of the intellect, activities of the mind. Yet the lock that gets placed is on the heart. This connects the intellectual with the spiritual. Who will exhaust their intellect studying this book and trying to understand its meaning for their own benefit? Only a person whose heart is sold on this book. Otherwise, people maintain a shallow view of the Quran, and immediately the disease in their hearts says, "This doesn't make sense. That seems unfair. How come the Quran says this? How come it says that?" This happens because there's no تَدَبُّر, no deep reflection, because the heart isn't engaged. The heart isn't in it.

translation: Indeed, those who reverted back [to disbelief] after guidance had become clear to them - Satan enticed them and prolonged hope for them
commentary:
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱرْتَدُّوا۟ عَلَىٰٓ أَدْبَـٰرِهِم مِّنۢ بَعْدِ مَا تَبَيَّنَ لَهُمُ ٱلْهُدَى ۙ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ سَوَّلَ لَهُمْ وَأَمْلَىٰ لَهُمْ

Those who turned back on their أَدْبَـٰر (backs) - notice the wordplay here with دُبُر from the previous ayah's تَدَبُّر. They turned on their backs مِّنۢ بَعْدِ مَا تَبَيَّنَ لَهُمُ ٱلْهُدَى (even after guidance had been made abundantly clear to them).

ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ سَوَّلَ لَهُمْ - Shaytan made things convenient for them. سَوَّلَ means to make something easy, to make it no big deal. Shaytan's successful strategy with them was convincing them: "What's the big deal?" Their evil deeds, their lack of following the Prophet ﷺ, their lack of seriousness about changing themselves and their faith, their lack of reflection on the Quran - Shaytan made all of it seem insignificant to them.

وَأَمْلَىٰ لَهُمْ - And he filled them with long-term worldly hopes. First, Shaytan made it easy for them to disregard the Quran - it became no big deal because the Quran offered them nothing compelling. Once that happened, the aspirations Allah ﷻ wants you to have - gardens with rivers flowing, homes in paradise, the company of the righteous - those aspirations stopped being presented to them regularly. 

So what aspirations filled their hearts instead? Only the ones Shaytan wanted them to have. The only future he offers concerns the dunya: buying a house, starting a business, getting married, moving to different cities, worldly dreams and plans. When the heart isn't regularly fed with visions of the eternal life Allah ﷻ promises, it becomes consumed with nothing but worldly long-term hopes.

translation: That is because they said to those who disliked what Allah sent down, "We will obey you in part of the matter." And Allah knows what they conceal
commentary:
ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ قَالُوا۟ لِلَّذِينَ كَرِهُوا۟ مَا نَزَّلَ ٱللَّهُ سَنُطِيعُكُمْ فِى بَعْضِ ٱلْأَمْرِ ۖ وَٱللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ إِسْرَارَهُمْ

That's because that group of hypocrites - those are the people who actually said to those who already hate what Allah ﷻ sent down... The Quraysh hated what Allah ﷻ revealed, and certain elements among the People of the Book hated what He revealed. So when the hypocrites heard about the impending battle, they sent a private message, an SOS, to Makkah saying: "Look, we know you're coming out to battle against Medina. The people of Medina have no real conflict with you - it's just this Muslim thing. And even though we're Muslim, we think they're being a bit extreme about this war. We want you to know we're on your side. If things don't work out for them, we're ready to support you."

سَنُطِيعُكُمْ فِى بَعْضِ ٱلْأَمْرِ (We will obey you in some matters) - they were backstabbing the Muslims while presenting negotiations to the enemies of Islam: "Of course we'll follow you in certain things. You're the leaders of Arabia, you're the powerful ones. Why wouldn't we follow you?"

This mirrors what we saw in Surah al-Baqarah when the hypocrites made secret alliances and the Muslims confronted them: "Why are you negotiating with Quraysh?" They responded, "No, we're just negotiating peace. What are you talking about?" And now Allah ﷻ exposes the kind of "peace" they were actually negotiating.

وَٱللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ إِسْرَارَهُمْ - And Allah ﷻ knows their إِسْرَار (deep secrets). إِسْرَار comes from سِرّ (secret), but إِسْرَار specifically means something you try extremely hard to keep hidden. There are ordinary secrets, but إِسْرَار refers to things you actively work to protect and conceal. Allah ﷻ already knew everything they were desperately trying to hide, and He exposed it in this ayah.

translation: Then how [will it be] when the angels take them in death, striking their faces and their backs
commentary:
فَكَيْفَ إِذَا تَوَفَّتْهُمُ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ يَضْرِبُونَ وُجُوهَهُمْ وَأَدْبَـٰرَهُمْ

How will things look when the angels take them away? يَضْرِبُونَ وُجُوهَهُمْ وَأَدْبَـٰرَهُمْ (striking their faces and their backs). The angel strikes them in the face. They grab their face in pain and fall forward, then the angel strikes them so hard on the back that they flip over. They try to get up, and he strikes them in the face again. This constant motion of pain - back and forth, face and back - as the angels execute their punishment.

translation: That is because they followed what angered Allah and disliked [what earns] His pleasure, so He rendered worthless their deeds
commentary:
ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمُ ٱتَّبَعُوا۟ مَآ أَسْخَطَ ٱللَّهَ وَكَرِهُوا۟ رِضْوَٰنَهُۥ فَأَحْبَطَ أَعْمَـٰلَهُمْ

That is because they followed what enraged Allah ﷻ. They pursued the very things that anger Him. وَكَرِهُوا۟ رِضْوَٰنَهُۥ (and they hated His pleasure) - they detested the things that would bring them Allah's pleasure and satisfaction. 

فَأَحْبَطَ أَعْمَـٰلَهُمْ (so He nullified their deeds). All their actions, whatever good they might have appeared to do, became worthless. When you follow what angers Allah ﷻ and reject what pleases Him, the inevitable result is that your deeds lose all value. They become hollow, devoid of any benefit in this life or the next.

translation: Or do those in whose hearts is disease think that Allah would never expose their [feelings of] hatred
commentary:
أَمْ حَسِبَ ٱلَّذِينَ فِى قُلُوبِهِم مَّرَضٌ أَن لَّن يُخْرِجَ ٱللَّهُ أَضْغَـٰنَهُمْ

The question Allah ﷻ poses here is penetrating: Have these people who carry disease in their hearts assumed that Allah will not bring out their grudges? The word أَضْغَـٰنَ  refers to deep-seated grudges that build into hatred—intense irritation and resentment that festers within.

These hypocrites harbor grudges against the Messenger ﷺ, against his instructions, against the changes unfolding in Madinah. They dislike the direction things are going, but they keep it hidden inside. Why? Because if they showed their true feelings, they couldn't maintain their Muslim identity anymore. And they need to stay Muslim—Muslims are the rising power, and the hypocrites want to remain relevant, to maintain their position and influence.

But Allah ﷻ is telling them: Do you think this disease you carry in your hearts will stay concealed? That I will not expose your grudges?

translation: And if We willed, We could show them to you, and you would know them by their mark; but you will surely know them by the tone of [their] speech. And Allah knows your deeds
commentary:
وَلَوْ نَشَآءُ لَأَرَيْنَـٰكَهُمْ فَلَعَرَفْتَهُم بِسِيمَـٰهُمْ ۚ وَلَتَعْرِفَنَّهُمْ فِى لَحْنِ ٱلْقَوْلِ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ أَعْمَـٰلَكُمْ

Allah ﷻ tells His Messenger ﷺ that if He wished, He would show him exactly who all these hypocrites are. This teaches us something important: Allah ﷻ did not reveal to the Messenger ﷺ the identity of all the hypocrites—only some of them.

Sometimes revelation would come exposing specific individuals. The Quran mentions ten people who attempted to assassinate the Prophet ﷺ. They were masked, covered in the darkness of night. They came as spies from within the Muslim community, but they failed. Through revelation, Allah ﷻ told the Prophet ﷺ exactly who they were by name. The Prophet ﷺ went to Hudhayfah (RA) and told him privately, "I'm going to tell you these names, but you cannot tell anyone else." Everyone knew that Hudhayfah (RA) was entrusted with this knowledge. Umar (RA) would later come to him asking, "Is my name on that list?" Hudhayfah (RA) would reassure him, "I can't tell you who they are, but I'll tell you—you're not among them."

Allah ﷻ says, "If We wished, We would have shown you who these people are—لَعَرَفْتَهُم بِسِيمَـٰهُمْ —you would have absolutely recognized them by their foreheads," as though Allah ﷻ would have given them a visible marking that the Prophet ﷺ could see.

But then comes the more practical instruction: وَلَتَعْرِفَنَّهُمْ فِى لَحْنِ ٱلْقَوْلِ —"But you will absolutely recognize them in the inflections of their speech." The phrase لَحْنِ ٱلْقَوْلِ  refers to speech pathology—the patterns, inflections, and nuances of how people speak. Allah ﷻ is telling the Prophet ﷺ: You can break apart their speech and tell from it where they truly stand. Their twisted words, their overly flowery language—if you listen carefully, you'll know exactly their real position.

Think of a shady car salesman trying to convince you of something unrealistic: "This is the most amazing thing since sliced bread! A 1975 Ford Taurus with only 350 miles—you're lucky I'm giving this to you for just $10,000!" If you're sharp, you see right through it.

The Prophet ﷺ is being told that he is a keen observer of human nature. From the way people raise and lower their voices, from their body language, from all their subtle cues—he can discern where they stand. This ayah reveals something profound about the Prophet's ﷺ character: his ability to read people when they open their mouths.

And through this, Allah ﷻ is teaching us not just to observe what people say, but how they say it. Body language matters. Speech patterns matter. Dissecting and analyzing communication is important.

The ayah concludes: وَٱللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ أَعْمَـٰلَكُمْ —"And Allah knows your deeds." Speech is one thing, actions are another. This is the mark of a hypocrite—their words are elaborate, but Allah ﷻ knows where their actions truly stand.

translation: And We will surely test you until We make evident those who strive among you [for the cause of Allah] and the patient, and We will test your affairs
commentary:
وَلَنَبْلُوَنَّكُمْ حَتَّىٰ نَعْلَمَ ٱلْمُجَـٰهِدِينَ مِنكُمْ وَٱلصَّـٰبِرِينَ وَنَبْلُوَا۟ أَخْبَارَكُمْ

Now the focus shifts: وَلَنَبْلُوَنَّكُمْ —"We will absolutely test you thoroughly." This is the point of cleansing, of purification. Allah ﷻ will test the believers حَتَّىٰ نَعْلَمَ ٱلْمُجَـٰهِدِينَ مِنكُمْ —"until We expose the real strugglers among you."

The word المجاهدين  refers to those who truly strive and struggle in Allah's path. Allah ﷻ will test until He reveals who among you are the genuine mujahidin, وَٱلصَّـٰبِرِينَ —and those who persevere regardless of circumstances.

The ayah ends with وَنَبْلُوَا۟ أَخْبَارَكُمْ —"We will test your news, your reports." When news comes, when events unfold, Allah ﷻ will observe how each person reacts. The test reveals your true character.

translation: Indeed, those who disbelieved and averted [people] from the path of Allah and opposed the Messenger after guidance had become clear to them - never will they harm Allah at all, and He will render worthless their deeds
commentary:
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَصَدُّوا۟ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ وَشَآقُّوا۟ ٱلرَّسُولَ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا تَبَيَّنَ لَهُمُ ٱلْهُدَىٰ لَن يَضُرُّوا۟ ٱللَّهَ شَيْـًٔا وَسَيُحْبِطُ أَعْمَـٰلَهُمْ
