This ayah echoes the opening of Surah Muhammad. There are two similar passages: the first appeared earlier in the surah, and this is the second. But notice the additions here.

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَصَدُّوا۟ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ —"Those who disbelieved and obstructed others from Allah's path." But now Allah ﷻ adds: وَشَآقُّوا۟ ٱلرَّسُولَ —"and opposed the Messenger." He doesn't even say "opposed Allah"—He says opposed the Messenger ﷺ specifically.

Then the crucial qualifier: مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا تَبَيَّنَ لَهُمُ ٱلْهُدَىٰ —"even after the guidance has been made abundantly clear to them."

The verdict is absolute: لَن يَضُرُّوا۟ ٱللَّهَ شَيْـًٔا —"They will not be able to harm Allah in any way, shape or form." وَسَيُحْبِطُ أَعْمَـٰلَهُمْ —"And soon Allah will take hold of all their deeds and nullify them."

translation: O you who have believed, obey Allah and obey the Messenger and do not invalidate your deeds
commentary:
۞ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ أَطِيعُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُوا۟ ٱلرَّسُولَ وَلَا تُبْطِلُوٓا۟ أَعْمَـٰلَكُمْ

After discussing those whose deeds are nullified, Allah ﷻ turns to the believers with a direct command: يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ —"O you who have iman!"

أَطِيعُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُوا۟ ٱلرَّسُولَ —"Obey Allah and obey the Messenger." This is profound. It's not "Obey Allah and obey the Messenger" as two separate commands. The structure in Arabic shows that obeying the Messenger ﷺ is the means of obeying Allah ﷻ. To obey Allah is to obey the Messenger ﷺ.

Then comes the warning: وَلَا تُبْطِلُوٓا۟ أَعْمَـٰلَكُمْ —"And do not cancel out your deeds."

These three surahs—Surah Muhammad, Surah Al-Fath and Surah Al-Hujurat—together form a comprehensive course on what it means to respect Allah's Messenger ﷺ. If someone wants to study our relationship with the Prophet ﷺ, what it means to be loyal to him, how to show him proper respect, and the critical importance of obeying him, these three surahs provide the complete framework. This is the "Muhammad section" of the Quran.

The believers are being told: You were with him in Makkah. You migrated with him. You were among the Ansar who helped him. You've stood with him on the battlefield. But if you disobey this Messenger ﷺ even once—deliberately, openly, defiantly—all that good you may have accumulated can be erased. Don't cancel out your good deeds.

translation: Indeed, those who disbelieved and averted [people] from the path of Allah and then died while they were disbelievers - never will Allah forgive them
commentary:
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَصَدُّوا۟ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ ثُمَّ مَاتُوا۟ وَهُمْ كُفَّارٌ فَلَن يَغْفِرَ ٱللَّهُ لَهُمْ

This ayah establishes an unalterable reality: إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَصَدُّوا۟ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ —"Those who disbelieved and stopped others from Allah's path," ثُمَّ مَاتُوا۟ وَهُمْ كُفَّارٌ —"then they died while they were in a state of disbelief and rejection."

The verdict: فَلَن يَغْفِرَ ٱللَّهُ لَهُمْ —"Then Allah will not be forgiving them."

When Allah ﷻ says He will not forgive someone, this occurs only rarely in the Quran, and only for two categories of people. First, Allah ﷻ says about shirk: إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَغْفِرُ أَن يُشْرَكَ بِهِۦ —"Indeed, Allah does not forgive that partners be associated with Him" (4:48). This applies to those who commit shirk and die upon it.

The only other place where Allah ﷻ declares He will not forgive is regarding the hypocrites. In Surah At-Tawbah, the Prophet ﷺ is told: whether you ask forgiveness for them or you don't, Allah will not forgive them.

Two groups: those who die upon shirk, and the hypocrites. This ayah applies to both—those whose disbelief was exposed and those whose disbelief remained hidden. The hidden form is nifaq (hypocrisy), the exposed form is open kufr. فَلَن يَغْفِرَ ٱللَّهُ لَهُمْ—Allah will not forgive them.

translation: So do not weaken and call for peace while you are superior; and Allah is with you and will never deprive you of [the reward of] your deeds
commentary:
فَلَا تَهِنُوا۟ وَتَدْعُوٓا۟ إِلَى ٱلسَّلْمِ وَأَنتُمُ ٱلْأَعْلَوْنَ وَٱللَّهُ مَعَكُمْ وَلَن يَتِرَكُمْ أَعْمَـٰلَكُمْ

After warning about nullified deeds, Allah ﷻ now addresses the believers with encouragement: فَلَا تَهِنُوا۟ —"Do not become weak, do not become soft."

This echoes a prophecy the Prophet ﷺ made about a future time when other nations would devour the Ummah like wolves devouring prey. He was asked, "Will it be because we are few in number?" He replied, "No, you will be many, but you will suffer from wahan (weakness)." The same word appears here.

وَتَدْعُوٓا۟ إِلَى ٱلسَّلْمِ —"And don't call to peace negotiations." The word السَّلْمِ  also means surrender. This is not the time to call for surrender or to seek peace from a position of weakness.

وَأَنتُمُ ٱلْأَعْلَوْنَ —"And you are the supreme ones." Not "you will be"—you are. Present tense. You already are in the superior position.

وَٱللَّهُ مَعَكُمْ —"And Allah is with you." وَلَن يَتِرَكُمْ أَعْمَـٰلَكُمْ —"And He will not take away the compensation of your deeds at all. He will not shortchange you in the slightest for any effort you make."

As the surah concludes, notice the contrast. Throughout, we've heard about those whose deeds are cancelled, seized, nullified, wasted. Now Allah ﷻ tells the believers: You stay on course, and Allah will not take away even a little bit from your deeds.

translation: [This] worldly life is only amusement and diversion. And if you believe and fear Allah, He will give you your rewards and not ask you for your properties
commentary:
إِنَّمَا ٱلْحَيَوٰةُ ٱلدُّنْيَا لَعِبٌ وَلَهْوٌ ۚ وَإِن تُؤْمِنُوا۟ وَتَتَّقُوا۟ يُؤْتِكُمْ أُجُورَكُمْ وَلَا يَسْـَٔلْكُمْ أَمْوَٰلَكُمْ

Allah ﷻ puts worldly life in perspective: إِنَّمَا ٱلْحَيَوٰةُ ٱلدُّنْيَا لَعِبٌ وَلَهْوٌ —"Worldly life amounts to nothing more than play and entertainment."

وَإِن تُؤْمِنُوا۟ وَتَتَّقُوا۟ —"But if you can firmly believe and truly protect yourselves from sin," يُؤْتِكُمْ أُجُورَكُمْ —"He will give you your full compensations."

Then comes a statement that requires careful understanding: وَلَا يَسْـَٔلْكُمْ أَمْوَٰلَكُمْ . This must be translated properly based on Arabic grammar. The structure here is jawab ash-shart (the consequence of a conditional statement). It means: "And when He gives you your compensation, He won't ask you for your wealth"—meaning in the Hereafter, not now.

This is where understanding Arabic grammar becomes critical. Some have mistranslated this as "He does not ask you for your wealth," which would be completely wrong. Allah ﷻ does ask for our wealth now—we're discussing battle, and what's the biggest part of any country's budget? The military. So if we're going into battle, of course Allah will ask for financial contributions.

But He's saying: If you maintain iman and taqwa, eventually you will receive your full compensation, and that will be the time you are no longer asked for anything.

translation: If He should ask you for them and press you, you would withhold, and He would expose your unwillingness
commentary:
إِن يَسْـَٔلْكُمُوهَا فَيُحْفِكُمْ تَبْخَلُوا۟ وَيُخْرِجْ أَضْغَـٰنَكُمْ

Now Allah ﷻ explains why He doesn't demand everything right now: إِن يَسْـَٔلْكُمُوهَا فَيُحْفِكُمْ —"If He were to ask you for all of it right now, it would be very difficult, a harsh burden on you."

تَبْخَلُوا۟ —"You would be stingy, you would hold back." وَيُخْرِجْ أَضْغَـٰنَكُمْ —"And your grudges would start coming out."

The word أَضْغَـٰنَ  appears again—the same grudges mentioned about the hypocrites. Allah ﷻ is saying: If I demanded all your wealth right now, even believers would start to complain. "What do you mean all my money? My house? All my savings? I can't give all of it!" Your grudges would surface.

translation: Here you are - those invited to spend in the cause of Allah - but among you are those who withhold [out of greed]. And whoever withholds only withholds [benefit] from himself; and Allah is the Free of need, while you are the needy. And if you turn away, He will replace you with another people; then they will not be the likes of you
commentary:
هَـٰٓأَنتُمْ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ تُدْعَوْنَ لِتُنفِقُوا۟ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ فَمِنكُم مَّن يَبْخَلُ ۖ وَمَن يَبْخَلْ فَإِنَّمَا يَبْخَلُ عَن نَّفْسِهِۦ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ ٱلْغَنِىُّ وَأَنتُمُ ٱلْفُقَرَآءُ ۚ وَإِن تَتَوَلَّوْا۟ يَسْتَبْدِلْ قَوْمًا غَيْرَكُمْ ثُمَّ لَا يَكُونُوٓا۟ أَمْثَـٰلَكُم

The surah concludes with a powerful statement about human nature and divine self-sufficiency.

هَـٰٓأَنتُمْ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ تُدْعَوْنَ لِتُنفِقُوا۟ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ —"Here you are, being invited to spend in Allah's path." فَمِنكُم مَّن يَبْخَلُ —"And among you are some who act cheaply and miserly."

وَمَن يَبْخَلْ فَإِنَّمَا يَبْخَلُ عَن نَّفْسِهِۦ —"Then whoever is cheap and miserly is only being miserly against his own self."

Muslims must understand: when you spend to support the Messenger's ﷺ mission, when you spend to make Islam's message reach others, when you support sincere efforts and causes—whether youth groups, dawah organizations, or any genuine Islamic work—those who ask you for support are not asking because they need it. You are not doing them a favor.

وَٱللَّهُ ٱلْغَنِىُّ —"Allah is free of need." He doesn't need anything from you. وَأَنتُمُ ٱلْفُقَرَآءُ —"And you are the ones who are bankrupt."

We are completely dependent on Allah ﷻ. But the full reality of our bankruptcy—when it will truly hit us—is when we stand before Allah ﷻ. Then our bankruptcy will become absolutely clear. The only people who will be happy that Day are those who actually have savings in the Hereafter. They have an account that doesn't just work in this dunya—they have an account that works in the akhirah.

Then comes one of the most sobering warnings in the Quran: وَإِن تَتَوَلَّوْا۟ —"And if you turn away," يَسْتَبْدِلْ قَوْمًا غَيْرَكُمْ —"He will replace you with another nation other than you."

Notice: يَسْتَبْدِلْ  means to replace little by little. Generation after generation, He will replace you. ثُمَّ لَا يَكُونُوٓا۟ أَمْثَـٰلَكُم —"Then they will not be like you."

They won't have your skin color. They won't speak your language. O Arabs, O Muslims who consider yourselves the custodians of Islam, the people of the language of the Quran—if you turn away, Allah ﷻ will replace you with a different ethnicity altogether, a different nation entirely.

لَا يَكُونُوٓا۟ أَمْثَـٰلَكُم—They will not be your likes. The word used is qawm (nation), not ummah. The ummah is eternal. But nations within the ummah are replaceable. The Arabs are a qawm. The Indians are a qawm. The Pakistanis, Bangladeshis, Egyptians, Syrians, Jordanians, Somalis, Sudanese—these are all replaceable to Allah ﷻ. The Ummah of Islam will remain, but individual nations can be replaced.

This is not just theory—it came true in our history. In 1258 CE, Baghdad was invaded, marking the end of the Abbasid Empire. The Tatars dragged the final Abbasid ruler in dead animal skin and beat him to death publicly. They massacred scholars, made piles of the heads of Muslim scholars in Baghdad, and sat on top of them eating their meals. The bloodshed was horrifying. We were essentially wiped out as a nation.

And then what happened? These very Tatars, these Mongols—their descendants became the Mamluks, the Safavids, the Seljuks, and finally the Ottomans whose khilafah we speak of so fondly. Literally: يَسْتَبْدِلْ قَوْمًا غَيْرَكُمْ ثُمَّ لَا يَكُونُوٓا۟ أَمْثَـٰلَكُم—"He will replace you with another nation, and they will not be like you."

You might think you're special, that your qawm is special. But no qawm is inherently special. The only thing that makes any group special is membership in this Ummah—not membership in a particular ethnic nation. Each qawm is replaceable.

This is a universal principle Allah ﷻ has given us. Look at the state of the Muslim community today, even in places like the United States. Those of us who come to Jumuah do not represent the vast majority of Muslims living here. And even among those who come to Jumuah, attendance is not an indication of taqwa. Coming to Jumuah indicates that something inside says you should come—perhaps because your father brought you, or from habit. It doesn't mean you're earning halal income. It doesn't mean you're praying five times daily. It doesn't mean anything definitive.

When we see numbers at Eid, we get happy. But the reality of the Ummah today, the actual state of affairs—not of the disbelievers but of the Muslims themselves—is frightening. We live in a comfortable religious bubble, and while that bubble has its comforts, sometimes you step outside it into reality and see what Muslims are really doing. The hookah lounges open late at night, filled with Muslims. The sororities run by Muslims on college campuses. If that's not turning away, what is? How much more can you turn away from the deen? What's left of Islam in such lives?

I remember living in Bay Shore, Long Island, and meeting a brother—a Puerto Rican man who had been Christian. He was walking down the street when he heard the adhan from the masjid two blocks away. Something stirred inside him: "I just want to know what this message is." He got a Spanish translation of the Quran and read it. He took his shahadah. He started reading the Quran from the beginning, and within a week reached Surah Al-Isra—halfway through the Quran. When he reached the ayah وَلَا تَقْرَبُوا۟ ٱلزِّنَىٰٓ —"Don't even go near fornication"—he was stunned. "I had no idea Islam doesn't allow this."

He had been living with his girlfriend and had a child with her. After reading that ayah, he started sleeping in the backyard. His girlfriend asked, "What's wrong with you? Why are you sleeping outside?" He said, "I can't talk about this right now. You'll understand eventually." He slept outside because he said, "If I go inside, it's too tempting."

Four weeks later, he came to me: "I have a problem. I had a relationship before Islam with this woman, and she's Christian. Is it okay for me to marry her?" I said, "Yes, absolutely." We did the nikah right there on his front porch, with his extended family present and a few brothers. We completed it right there.

I look at people like that and think: when Muslims turn away from this deen, Allah ﷻ replaces them one by one with people who value this deen for what it is, who appreciate it for what it is. They don't take it for granted. They don't view it as something annoying their parents passed down. They recognize its immense value.

I've been to African American Muslim communities where they're reciting English translations of the Quran and crying their hearts out. Just English translations—and that's enough. That's how inclined their hearts are to the word of Allah ﷻ.

We are being replaced, literally. Nations are being replaced. يَسْتَبْدِلْ قَوْمًا غَيْرَكُمْ ثُمَّ لَا يَكُونُوٓا۟ أَمْثَـٰلَكُم—He will replace you with a different people, and they will not be like you.

May Allah ﷻ not make us among those who get replaced. May He make us among those who change ourselves and never become those who turn back from His deen.
